elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that the period function of the center at the origin of the $\mathbb{Z _k$-equivariant differential equation $\dot{z =iz+a(z\overline{z )^nz^{k+1 , a\ne0,$ is monotonous decreasing for all $n$ and $k$ positive integers, solving a conjecture about them.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: We show this result as corollary of proving that the period function of the center at the origin of a sub-family of the reversible quadratic centers is monotonous decreasing as well.
elements.sent-2411-003: Real planar autonomous analytic differential systems can be written in complex coordinates in the compact form $\dot{z =F(z,\overline{z )$, $z\in\mathbb{C .$ The $\mathbb{Z _k$-equivariant differential equations are equations of this form that are invariant under a rotation through the angle $2\pi/k$ about the origin.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: They are studied in detail in the classical book of Arnold \cite[Chap.
elements.sent-2411-005: For them, the phase portrait on each sector of width $2\pi/k$ centered at the origin is repeated $k$ times.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper we are concerned with the simplest family of polynomial $\mathbb{Z _k$-equivariant differential equations with a non-degenerated center at the origin and homogeneous nonlinearities \begin{equation}\label{Zk} \dot{z}=iz+a(z\overline{z})^nz^{k+1},\end{equation} with $n$ and $k$ a positive integers and $0\ne a\in\mathbb{C .$ It is also a family of differential equations given by simply two complex monomials, as the ones studied in~\cite{AGP .}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: The qualitative properties of the period function associated to the origin was posed as an open problem in \cite[Prob.. 16]{Gasull .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, it is conjectured that it is monotonous decreasing.
elements.sent-2411-009: The main result of this paper is a positive answer to such conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover it starts being $2\pi$ at the origin and tends to $2(k+n)\pi/(k+2n)$ when the orbits approach to the boundary of the period annulus.
elements.sent-2411-011: It is either constant, decreasing towards $0,$ or increasing towards infinity, see Proposition~\ref{lem:nou .}
elements.sent-2411-012: As shown in \cite{Gasull , the period function of the center at the origin of~\eqref{Zk is strongly related to the period function of the center at the origin of the family of reversible quadratic centers \begin{equation}\label{loud0} \begin{cases} \dot{x}=-y+xy, \dot{y}=x+Dx^2+Fy^2. \end{cases}\end{equation} In particular, as we also will see in Section~\ref{sec:ProofThmA , with the period function for the period annulus of the origin for the sub-family \begin{equation}\label{loud} \begin{cases} \dot{x}=-y+xy, \dot{y}=x+Dx^2+(D+1)y^2, \end{cases}\end{equation} with $D\in(-1/2,0)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Among the quadratic centers, the period function of the reversible family~\eqref{loud0 is the one with richer qualitative properties.}
elements.sent-2411-014: As the counterpart of the renowned conjecture claiming that quadratic systems have at most four limit cycles, it was conjectured in \cite{Chicone2 that quadratic centers have at most two critical periods (that is, number of maxima and minima of the period function).}
elements.sent-2411-015: Since then, the qualitative behavior of the period function of the reversible quadratic centers has attracted the attention of the community \cite{MMV0,MMV,MV .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, the family~\eqref{loud with $D\in(-1/2,0)$ was believed to have monotonous decreasing period function, see \cite{MMV0,MMV .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In fact, a first indication that the period function could be decreasing is given the computation of its first period constant $P_2,$ which gives its local behavior near the origin.
elements.sent-2411-018: More specifically, if ${\bf T (\rho), \rho>0,$ denotes the minimal period of the orbit starting at $(\rho,0),$ then \begin{equation}\label{eq:t2} {\bf T}(\rho)=2\pi +P_2\rho^2+O(\rho^3).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-019: For system~\eqref{loud0 it is known that \begin{equation}P_2= \frac\pi{12}\big(10D^2+10DF-D+4F^2-5F+1\big),\end{equation} see for instance~\cite{MMV .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Hence, when $F=D+1$ some computations give than $P_2=\pi D(2D+1),$ proving that $P_2<0$ when $D\in(-1/2,0)$ and hence that $T(\rho)$ is locally decreasing at the origin.
elements.sent-2411-021: The period annulus of the origin of system~\eqref{loud is all the half-plane $\mathcal{B :=\{(x,y)\,:\,x<1\}.$ It is proved in~\cite[Sec 5]{MMV0 that the period function is also decreasing at the boundary of~$\mathcal B,$ result that is again coherent with the fact that the period function is decreasing.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: There exist a large collection of criteria to verify the monotonicity of the period in the literature, \cite{Chicone,GGV,Villadelprat,Schaaf are some examples.}
elements.sent-2411-023: During the last years, the attempts to prove the monotonicity of the period for the family of quadratic centers~\eqref{loud with $D\in(-1/2,0)$ have been numerous and unfortunately unfruitful despite all efforts.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In particular, to the best of our knowledge, all known criteria have been checked to fail for $D\in(-1,0)\setminus\{-1/2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-025: A similar case where monotonicity holds in system~\eqref{loud0 is when $F=1-2D,$ \cite{Cho .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: To see other regions of monotonicity of the period function see~\cite{Villadelprat0 and its references.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Recently, in \cite{Liu a new adaptation of the criteria introduced in \cite{Villadelprat has been used to prove the monotonicity of another different but similar family of quadratic centers, the so-called reversible Lotka-Volterra.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: These are, reversible quadratic centers that belong also to the family of generalized Lotka-Volterra quadratic centers.
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, they consist on the sub-family $F=-D$.
elements.sent-2411-030: By using this criteria we prove the following.
elements.sent-2411-031: If $D\in(-\infty,-1)\cup(0,+\infty)$ the period annulus is the interior of the homoclinic orbit of the equilibrium at $(-1/D,0)$.
elements.sent-2411-032: The period starts being $2\pi$ at the origin and tends to $+\infty$ when the orbits approach the boundary of the period annulus.
elements.sent-2411-033: If $D\in[-1,0]$ the period annulus is all the half-plane $\mathcal{B =\{(x,y)\,:\,x<1\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, when $D\ne0,$ the period starts being $2\pi$ at the origin and tends\footnote{When $D=-1$ this limit must be understood as infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-035: There are results in the Theorem that are already known and we included them for completeness.
elements.sent-2411-036: Indeed, the shape of the period annulus for each value of the parameter is given in \cite{MMV and the monotonicity for $D\in(-\infty,-1)\cup(0,+\infty)\setminus\{1\}$ was proved in \cite[Thm A]{Villadelprat0 .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, the case $D=1$ was studied in \cite{Cho as part of the proof of monotonicity for $F=2$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Also, it is well-known that the only isochronous systems in~\eqref{loud0 are \begin{equation}(D,F)\in\{(-1/2,1/2), (-1/2,2), (0,1/4), (0,1) \},\end{equation} see for instance~\cite{CS .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Notice that the cases $D=-1/2$ and $D=0$ in system~\eqref{loud precisely correspond to two of them.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In short, our contribution then is the completeness of the study of the monotonicity of the straight line $F=D+1$ to the segment $D\in[-1,0)$, which was unknown to the best of our knowledge (with the exception of the isochronous center).
elements.sent-2411-041: As we have explained, our proof on this segment is based on the general criterion for potential systems commented above.
elements.sent-2411-042: This criterion is introduced in next section and all the rest of the proof is self-contained.
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, in Subsection~\ref{se:nova we study the period function of the other center in family~\eqref{loud and use another idea that allows to reduce the study of the period function on the above segment to a segment of the family $D=-F,$ providing an alternative proof based totally on the main result of~\cite{Liu .}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-045: In Section 2 we introduce the monotonicity criterium from~\cite{Liu .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Sections 3 and 4 are devoted to the proofs of Theorems~\ref{thmB and~\ref{thmA , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Consider a Hamiltonian system with a Hamiltonian function of the form $H(x,y)={y^2 /{2 +V(x)$ and integrating factor $\ell(x)$, where both $V$ and $\ell$ are analytic functions in a neighborhood of $x=0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: That is, the differential system is written as \begin{equation}\label{system} \dot{x}= -\dfrac{y}{\ell(x)}, \; \dot{y}= \dfrac{V'(x)}{\ell(x)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: If $V'(0)=0$ and $xV'(x)>0$ for all $x\approx 0$, $x\neq 0$, the origin is a center.
elements.sent-2411-050: The period annulus is the largest punctured neighborhood of the origin foliated by periodic orbits and each periodic orbit of the period annulus can be parametrized by an energy level $h\mapsto\gamma(h)\subset\{(x,y)\in\mathbb{R ^2:H(x,y)=h\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The period function of the center assigns to each periodic orbit of the period annulus its minimal period.
elements.sent-2411-052: Due to the previous parameterization and the particular expression of the vector field, the period function can be expressed as an Abelian integral function of the energy, \begin{equation}\label{period} T(h):=\int_0^{T(h)}\,\operatorname{d}\!t = \int_{\gamma(h)} \frac{\ell(x)}{y}\,\operatorname{d}\!x.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: Notice that $T(h)={\bf T \big(\sqrt{2h}\,\big),$ where ${\bf T}$ is given in~\eqref{eq:t2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Let us denote by $\mathcal{I =(x_{\ell ,x_r)$ the projection on the $x$-axis of the period annulus of the center at the origin, with $x_{\ell <0<x_r$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Since $xV'(x)>0$ for all $x\in\mathcal{I}\setminus\{0\}$, the potential function $V$ defines an analytic involution $\sigma$ on $\mathcal{I}$ by \begin{equation}V(\sigma(x))=V(x)  for all x\inI\setminus\{0\},\end{equation} and $\sigma(0)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-056: The following criterium is introduced in \cite[Prop.
elements.sent-2411-057: If $\mathbf{\Pi _{\sigma (g)(x)$ has no zeros in $(0,x_r)$ then $I'(h)$ has no zeros.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: It can be seen that, by taking $g=\ell$, the corresponding $\mathbf{\Pi _{\sigma (\ell)(x)$ changes sign in $(0,x_r)$.}}

elements.sent-2411-128: In particular, \begin{equation}P_2(u,w;D):=uw(1+Du)^3(1+Dw)^3F_2(u,w;D)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}P_3(u,w;D):=(u-1)u^2(1+Du)^4(1+Dw)P(w;D)F_3(u,w;D)/w,\end{equation} with $P_2(u,w;D)$ a polynomial of degree $7$ and $P_3(u,w;D)$ a polynomial of degree $11$.
elements.sent-2411-129: We omit their expressions for the sake of brevity.
elements.sent-2411-130: Consequently, common zeros of $F_2(u,w;D)=0$ and $F_3(u,w;D)=0$ are equivalent to common zeros of $P_2(u,w;D)=0$ and $P_3(u,w;D)=0$.
elements.sent-2411-131: We now perform the resultant of $P_2(u,w;D)$, $P_3(u,w;D)$ with respect to $w$.
elements.sent-2411-132: That is, \begin{equation}Res(P_2(u,w;D),P_3(u,w;D),w)=S(u;D)R_2(u;D),\end{equation} with \begin{equation}S(u;D)=-8D^9u^7(u-1)^3(1+2D)(D+1)^9(Du+1)^{21}(D(1+2D)u^2+2Du+1),\end{equation} and $R_2(u;D)$ a polynomial of degree $12$ in $u$.

elements.sent-2411-134: Let us show that $R_2(u;D)$ does not have zeros for $(u,D)\in(0,1)\times(-1/2,0)$ either.
elements.sent-2411-135: We will proceed with a bifurcation argument with respect to the parameter $D$.. First, \begin{equation}\label{R201} R_2(0;D)=54D(D+1) \text{ and } R_2(1;D)=4D(1+2D)^4(D+1)^9.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-136: Thus no roots enter or escape from the interval $(0,1)$ as we change the value of $D$ within its range.

elements.sent-2411-138: Let us locate the zeros of $\Delta_u(D)$ on $(-{1 /{2 ,0)$.}}

elements.sent-2411-140: The three roots $D_0$, $D_1$ and $D_2$ split the interval $(-1/2,0)$ in four sub-intervals where $R_2(u;D)$ has the same number of roots for any $D$ on each sub-interval.

elements.sent-2411-142: Taking rational representative of $D$ on each sub-interval and using Sturm's theorem we can check that $R_2(u;D)$ has no zeros in $(0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-143: The last part of the proof is devoted to show that $R_2(u;D_i),$ $i=0,1,2,$ has no zeros in $(0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-144: Sturm's theorem can be directly applied to $R_2(u;D_i)$, $i=0,1$, by using it over the algebraic extension $\mathbb{Q [D_i]$.}
elements.sent-2411-145: For $R_2(u;D_2)$ the same argument can be done, but $D_2$ is implicitly defined by a degree $14$ polynomial, so the computations are more cumbersome.
elements.sent-2411-146: We avoid that by using an alternative argument, which could be also have been applied for the previous two cases.
elements.sent-2411-147: We first notice from the identities~\eqref{R201 that $R_2(u;D_2)$ is negative for $u=0$ and $u=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-148: In order to show that $R_2(u;D_2)<0$ for all $u\in(0,1)$ we locate the root $D_2$ inside a suitable rational interval\footnote{These rational upper and lower bounds of $D_2$ are obtained by taking two suitable successive convergents of its continued fraction representation.. \begin{equation}D_2 \in[\delta,\overline{\delta}]:=[-16/125,-267/2086]\approx [-0.128,-0.127996].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Thus, we can construct a polynomial $U(u)$ with rational coefficients satisfying $R_2(u;D_2)<U(u)$ by upper-bounding each monomial of $R_2(u;D)$ with $D=\delta$ or $D=\overline{\delta}$ depending on the sign of the coefficient and the exponent of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-151: Finally, Sturm's theorem can be used again to show that $U(u)$ is negative in $(0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-153: This ends with the proof that $\psi_1(u;D)\neq\psi_2(u;D)$ for any $D\in(-1/2,0)$ and ends the proof of the Claim.
elements.sent-2411-154: As we mentioned at the beginning of the proof, the monotonicity of the period function for $D\in(-1,-1/2)$ can be proved in similar fashion.
elements.sent-2411-155: The main difference with respect to the previous is the treatment of the system given by equations $P_2(u,w;D)=0$ and $P_3(u,w;D)=0.$ While in the first we start studying it by computing the resultant of both polynomials with respect to $w,$ in this case, it is more convenient the compute the resultant with respect to $u$.
elements.sent-2411-156: We omit the details for the sake of shortness.
elements.sent-2411-157: To finish the proof for $D\in(-1,0)$ it remains to study the behavior of the period function at the origin and at the exterior boundary of the period annulus.
elements.sent-2411-158: That it tends to $2\pi$ at the origin is a well-known fact because it is the period of the linear part of the differential equation, see also~\eqref{eq:t2 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: On the other hand, to show that at the exterior boundary is at least $\pi/(D+1)$ it suffices to observe that $x=1$ is an invariant straight line for the flow of equation~\eqref{loud and that the restricted flow on this line is given by $\dot y= (D+1)(1+y^2).$ Hence, the time $\mathcal{T}$ spend by the flow to travel along all this line is \begin{equation}\label{eq:t} \mathcal{T}=\frac1{D+1}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac {1}{1+y^2}\,\operatorname{d}\! y=\frac{\pi}{D+1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-160: By continuous dependence with respect initial conditions the total period of the periodic orbits near the boundary tends to $\mathcal{T}$ plus the time spend to close the orbit.
elements.sent-2411-162: We end the proof with the special case $D=-1$.
elements.sent-2411-164: It has a straight line $\{x=1\}$ of singularities and $H(x,y)=x^2+y^2$ is a first integral of the system.
elements.sent-2411-165: The period starts being $2\pi$ at the origin as-well, and it tends to infinity as approaching the boundary of the period annulus with the singularity at $(1,0)$.
elements.sent-2411-166: In particular, the period function can be explicitly computed by changing to polar coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-167: Indeed, setting $r^2=x^2+y^2$ and denoting $T(r)$ the minimal period of the orbit with radius $r$, \begin{equation}T(r)=\int_0^{2\pi} \frac 1{1-r\cos \theta}\,\operatorname{d}\! \theta=\frac{2\pi}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-168: This proves the monotonicity and it ends the proof of the theorem.
elements.subsection-2411-001: About the period function for the other center of system~\eqref{loud}

elements.sent-2411-188: Firstly, since $a\ne0$ it is easy to see that it is not restrictive to reduce the study to the case $a=1.$ Next, we can use polar coordinates $z=re^{i\theta}$ to write equation~\eqref{Zk with $a=1$ as \begin{equation}\label{sys_rt} \frac{\operatorname{d}\! r}{\operatorname{d}\!t} = r^{2n+k+1}\cos(k\theta), \,\, \frac{\operatorname{d}\!\theta}{\operatorname{d}\!t}=1+r^{2n+k}\sin(k\theta).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-189: The change of variables $\{R=r^{2n+k ,\Theta=k\theta\}$ and the rescaling of time $\tau=kt$ by the constant factor $k>0$ brings the previous to the system \begin{equation}\frac{\operatorname{d}\!R}{\operatorname{d}\!\tau}=b R^2\cos\Theta, \,\, \frac{\operatorname{d}\!\Theta}{\operatorname{d}\!\tau} = 1+ R\sin\Theta,\end{equation} where $b:=1+{2n /{k}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Rewriting the system in cartesian coordinates $X+iY=Re^{i\Theta}$, we obtain \begin{equation}\label{sys_XY} \frac{\operatorname{d}\!X}{\operatorname{d}\!\tau}=-Y+bX^2-Y^2, \, \, \frac{\operatorname{d}\!Y}{\operatorname{d}\!\tau}=X+(1+b)XY.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-191: Now, using the change of variables given by $\{x=-(1+b)Y,y=-(1+b)X\}$ and changing the sign of time $s=-\tau$ we arrive to \begin{equation}\label{sys_xy} \frac{\operatorname{d}\!x}{\operatorname{d}\!s} = -y+xy, \, \, \frac{\operatorname{d}\!y}{\operatorname{d}\!s} = x-\frac{1}{1+b}x^2+\frac{b}{1+b}y^2,\end{equation} which is system~\eqref{loud with $D=-1/(1+b)=-k/(2(k+n))\in(-1/2,0).$ As we can see from the periodicity of system~\eqref{sys_rt with respect to $\theta$, system \eqref{Zk is $\mathbb{Z _k$-equivariant.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: In particular, the period of a periodic orbit of~\eqref{Zk around the origin is $k$ times the time spent by the orbit of \eqref{sys_rt from $\theta=0$ to $\theta={2\pi /{k .$ By the construction of system~\eqref{sys_XY , this time is exactly $1/k$ times the period of the corresponding periodic orbit around the origin of~\eqref{sys_XY , which equals the period of the periodic orbit around the origin of~\eqref{sys_xy .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Thus, we have that the period function at the origin of system~\eqref{Zk equals the period function at the origin of system~\eqref{loud with $D=-k/(2(k+n))\in(-1/2,0).$ Therefore, by Theorem~\ref{thmB , the period function of the center at the origin of the quadratic reversible center~\eqref{loud when $D\in(-1/2,0$) is monotonous decreasing.}}}}
